As user behaviour evolves — new terminology emerges, query patterns shift, new use cases appear — the model's specialisation becomes misaligned with current production traffic. This manifests as gradual quality degradation with no obvious cause. The defence is continuous evaluation: run your eval suite on a sample of recent production traffic weekly and track scores over time. When you detect drift, the response is re-collection of training data from the current production distribution and a new fine-tuning run. Fine-tuning is not a one-time operation — it is a continuous process that must keep pace with the evolution of your user base.

ch15.question: You are building a multi-tenant agent SaaS. Tenant A is a law firm; Tenant B is a software startup. Both use the same underlying agent code, but their agents must use different system prompts, tool configurations, and knowledge bases. What architectural pattern correctly supports this?
options: Deploy a completely separate agent instance per tenant — shared code creates security risks

Use a tenant context object injected at request time that parametrises system prompt templates, tool availability lists, and knowledge base namespaces; a single agent factory function produces a fully configured agent per request from this context
Allow tenants to upload their own system prompts and merge them with the base prompt at runtime
Use feature flags to enable or disable capabilities per tenant — the simplest approach is always best
ch15.explanation: The tenant context pattern is the standard solution to multi-tenancy in agentic systems. A TenantContext object carries all configuration that varies per tenant: system prompt template variables, allowed tool list, knowledge base namespace, rate limits, and model preferences. A factory function consumes this context and constructs a fully configured Agent object — the same code path serves every tenant, but each agent is correctly scoped to its tenant's configuration. This is more maintainable than separate deployments (one codebase), more secure than user-uploaded prompts (templates with validated substitution only), and more powerful than feature flags (supports arbitrary configuration, not just binary toggles). Crucially, tool lists and knowledge base namespaces prevent cross-tenant data access.

ch15.question: Your agent SaaS charges customers based on usage. A customer on the $99/month plan calls your most expensive tool (which invokes a $0.05 Claude Opus call) 2,000 times in one day, generating $100 in costs against $99 in revenue. What production capability was missing that would have prevented this?
options: The pricing model was wrong — the solution is to raise prices

Usage metering with per-tenant rate limits: track tool invocations and token consumption per tenant in real time, enforce configurable limits that match each tier's economics, and return a graceful degradation response (not an error) when a tenant approaches their limit
The expensive tool should have been restricted to higher-tier plans only
Implement a circuit breaker that stops all agent activity if daily costs exceed a threshold
ch15.explanation: Usage metering is a foundational requirement for any SaaS product with variable-cost AI operations. The system must track consumption per tenant in real time (not just at billing cycle end), enforce limits that are calibrated to each tier's unit economics, and respond gracefully when limits are approached — ideally warning the user before hard-cutting them off. The correct implementation uses an atomic increment in a fast store (Redis INCR with a TTL matching the metering window) so that every tool invocation is tracked without race conditions. A circuit breaker that stops all activity is too blunt; a well-designed rate limiter applies per-tool and per-model limits so that cheap operations remain available even when expensive ones are throttled.

ch15.question: A hiring manager asks you to explain what distinguishes an 'agent developer' from a 'prompt engineer' or 'ML engineer'. What is the most accurate description of the distinct skill set?
options: Agent developers write more Python code than prompt engineers, who primarily write English

Agent developers operate at the intersection of software engineering, LLM behaviour, and systems design: they must reason about distributed systems, design tool interfaces, engineer prompts that produce reliable reasoning, build evaluation infrastructure, and think about security threat models — none of which is the primary domain of either prompt engineers or ML engineers
Agent developers are ML engineers who have learned to use the Anthropic API
The roles are equivalent — the distinction is primarily marketing terminology
ch15.explanation: Agent development is a genuinely distinct discipline that sits at an intersection that did not exist before agentic AI. A prompt engineer's primary skill is crafting natural language instructions — valuable, but insufficient when the agent must make dozens of tool calls across a multi-hour workflow. An ML engineer's primary skill is model training and infrastructure — valuable, but most agent developers do not train their own models. The agent developer must hold multiple disciplines simultaneously: software engineering for tool and orchestration code, LLM behaviour understanding for reliable prompt design, systems thinking for state management and error handling, security awareness for threat modelling, and evaluation discipline for measuring quality. This combination is why the role commands a premium and why it is difficult to hire for.

ch15.question: You are designing an agent system for a new domain. Before writing any code, you apply the five-question design framework. The answer to 'What does done look like?' is vague — the stakeholders disagree on what constitutes a successful outcome. What should you do before proceeding?
options: Proceed with development using the most reasonable interpretation of done, then adjust based on stakeholder feedback after launch

This is a red flag that must be resolved before the first line of code is written. A vague definition of done means you cannot build an eval suite, cannot set stopping conditions, and cannot know when the agent is ready to ship — resolve it with stakeholders now
Define done yourself as the developer — technical definitions are more precise than stakeholder definitions
Build a prototype first; seeing the agent work in practice will help stakeholders articulate what they want
ch15.explanation: Every other design decision depends on a clear definition of done. Stopping conditions require it: the agent needs to know when the task is complete. Evaluation requires it: you cannot measure success without a success criterion. Guardrail design requires it: you cannot define what the agent should refuse to do if you do not know what it is supposed to do. A prototype built without a definition of done will be evaluated subjectively, producing inconsistent feedback and endless revision cycles. The five-question framework forces this clarity before code is written precisely because resolving ambiguity is infinitely cheaper in specification than in implementation. If stakeholders cannot agree, facilitate a working session to produce a written, measurable definition — then proceed.

ch15.question: Looking across all 15 chapters, which single principle most consistently separates agents that succeed in production from those that fail?
options: Using the largest, most capable model available for every operation
Instrumenting the system with comprehensive observability from the start, so that when problems occur — and they will — you can diagnose root causes rather than guessing
Implementing fine-tuning before deployment to ensure the model behaves correctly
Building multi-agent architectures from day one so the system can scale without redesign
ch15.explanation: Every chapter in this course has touched on observability: structured logging in deployment, eval frameworks in evaluation, latency profiling in streaming, drift detection in fine-tuning, audit trails in security. The consistent pattern across production failures is not model capability, architecture choice, or infrastructure — it is the inability to diagnose what went wrong. An agent with imperfect performance but comprehensive observability can be systematically improved. An agent with excellent benchmark performance but no observability is a black box that degrades unpredictably. The best model is not the largest; it is the one whose behaviour you understand. The best architecture is not the most sophisticated; it is the one you can observe, measure, and improve. Observability is the meta-skill that makes all other skills compounding.
